tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying congenital prothrombin deficiency is the benchmark outcome for an L1 diagnosis task and anchors appropriate counseling and future management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency (or equivalent phrasing) and links it to impaired clotting.

Explains bleeding risk in plain language (e.g., easy bruising, mucosal bleeding, prolonged bleeding after procedures) and gives at least one example of when to seek urgent care (e.g., head injury, heavy/ongoing bleeding).
Acknowledges the condition is congenital/rare and distinguishes it from vitamin K deficiency/warfarin effect (acquired causes) at least briefly.
Mentions treatment options for bleeding or procedures and must include both PCC and FFP as potential replacement therapies (contextualized as clinician-directed/acute management).

Performs the required safety checks before any prescribing: checks contraindications (critical) and also checks allergies and drug interactions if prescribing is attempted; if no prescribing occurs, does not fabricate a completed check.
Avoids unsafe advice: does not recommend anticoagulants, antiplatelet therapy, or NSAIDs without caution; advises avoiding aspirin/NSAIDs unless specifically directed.
Recommends hematology involvement/follow-up planning conceptually (even if not using a referral tool in this L1 task) and addresses family/genetic considerations when appropriate.
Uses clear, non-alarming communication and confirms understanding (e.g., invites questions or teach-back).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency based on bleeding history and supportive coagulation pattern, then provide brief counseling including bleeding risk and key treatment options (PCC/FFP).
must_collect: personal bleeding history (epistaxis, gum bleeding, menorrhagia, easy bruising, post-procedure bleeding)
history of surgeries/dental work and bleeding outcomes
medication history (anticoagulants, antiplatelets, NSAIDs) and vitamin K exposure
basic lab pattern awareness (prolonged PT/INR with possible aPTT prolongation; correction on mixing study; low factor II activity if known)
steps: Elicit bleeding phenotype severity and triggers; assess for red flags (intracranial bleed symptoms, GI bleed, postpartum bleeding).
Review medication and dietary factors to rule out acquired causes (warfarin/vitamin K deficiency/liver disease) and ask about prior abnormal PT/INR.
Synthesize findings consistent with congenital factor II deficiency and state the diagnosis.
Explain diagnosis and bleeding risk; provide safety-net/urgent return precautions.
Explain treatment at a high level for bleeding/procedures and include PCC and FFP as options (noting these are clinician-administered).
Document the diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

steps: Gather bleeding and family history; review meds and comorbidities suggesting acquired coagulopathy.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of juvenile idiopathic arthritis associated with LACC1 defect, enabling appropriate counseling and downstream management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis as juvenile idiopathic arthritis and explicitly links it to a LACC1-related (monogenic) cause in a clear, patient/family-appropriate explanation (must include the terms "juvenile idiopathic arthritis" and "LACC1").

Demonstrates diagnostic reasoning consistent with inflammatory arthritis: describes chronic joint pain/swelling and morning stiffness, functional limitation, and/or warmth/effusion; distinguishes inflammatory from mechanical pain.

Screens for red flags that would change urgency or indicate alternative diagnoses (e.g., fever/toxic appearance, severe bone pain, inability to bear weight, rapidly progressive monoarthritis suggesting septic arthritis, recent trauma).

Acknowledges uncertainty and the need for confirmation/monitoring when appropriate (e.g., trend inflammatory markers, imaging, ophthalmology screening for uveitis if JIA phenotype warrants), without contradicting the recorded diagnosis.

Provides a basic, safe initial management overview without prescribing (L1): supportive care (NSAID use only if no contraindications), activity modification, and importance of rheumatology follow-up; avoids initiating DMARDs/biologics via prescribing tool in this task.
Communication milestone coverage: explains diagnosis in plain language, includes expected course, and addresses family questions; avoids blame and explains genetic contribution neutrally.

Does not make unsafe medication recommendations; if discussing future disease-modifying therapy, frames it as specialist-directed and includes mention that methotrexate with folic acid is a common option (without issuing a prescription in an L1 task).

If any medication is suggested in narrative, performs or prompts required safety checks before prescribing (allergy check; contraindication and drug interaction checks are critical) and does not proceed with prescribing in the absence of those checks.

steps: Elicit history focusing on chronicity, inflammatory features, joint pattern, and impact on function; ask about systemic symptoms and eye symptoms.
